file_name: Nagendram et al. - 2023 - Stochastic gradient descent optimisation for convolutional neural network for medical image segmenta.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep-convolutional neural network (CNN) integrated with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization for medical image segmentation. Specifically, it applied this AI method to dermoscopic clinical images from the PH2 dataset for melanoma lesion segmentation, comparing its performance against U-net and FCN architectures.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the proposal of a novel deep-CNN-integrated methodology with stochastic gradient descent optimization, implementation on the PH2 dataset, and reporting of specific performance metrics (sensitivity, accuracy, dice coefficient) compared against U-net and FCN architectures.

title: Stochastic gradient descent optimisation for convolutional neural network for medical image segmentation
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel deep-convolutional neural network (CNN) integrated with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization for medical image segmentation, specifically targeting dermoscopic images from the PH2 dataset for melanoma lesion segmentation. The proposed architecture, which incorporates Jaccard distance and binary cross-entropy loss functions, was evaluated against U-net and FCN baselines using sensitivity, accuracy, and Dice coefficient metrics. Experimental results on the PH2 dataset demonstrated that the proposed method outperformed existing architectures, achieving a sensitivity of 0.972, accuracy of 0.981, and Dice coefficient of 0.773. Additionally, the model showed robust performance on chest-X-ray images, achieving a sensitivity of 0.993 and accuracy of 0.9887. The study concludes that the SGD-optimized CNN provides superior segmentation efficiency and boundary clarity compared to conventional methods, particularly in handling illumination variations and hair artifacts.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study utilized the PH2 dataset, which contains dermoscopic images for melanoma lesion segmentation. The paper explicitly states the data source is the 'PH2 Database for Melanoma lesion segmentation' and evaluates the model on these images.

all_task_types.target: Lesion masks for melanoma in dermoscopic images and lung structures/lesions in chest-X-ray images.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'medical image segmentation' using a deep-convolutional neural network (CNN). The model predicts binary masks for regions of interest (ROI), specifically melanoma lesions in the PH2 dataset and lung structures/lesions in chest-X-ray images. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics such as Dice coefficient, sensitivity, and accuracy, comparing the proposed method against U-net and FCN architectures.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for melanoma lesion segmentation, obtained from the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano in Matosinhos, Portugal.

use.training: Used for training the proposed SGD-optimized CNN model (160 images).
use.validation: Used for validation (40 images, 20% of training set size).
use.testing: Used for testing (50 images, 25% of label mask set scope).

datasets_used.name: Chest-Xray Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of chest-Xray images made available via Kaggle, used for segmenting lung structures and lesions.

use.training: Used for training the algorithm on different medical images.
use.other: Used for external comparison against U-net and FCN architectures.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed SGD-optimized CNN and baseline architectures (U-net, FCN) were made using descriptive metrics (Sensitivity, Accuracy, Dice coefficient) without statistical significance testing.

PH2 Dataset.notes: The proposed method outperformed U-net and FCN architectures. The results are presented in Table 1 of the paper.

Chest-Xray Dataset.notes: The proposed method outperformed U-net and FCN architectures. The results are presented in Table 2 of the paper. Additionally, the abstract and conclusion sections mention specific performance metrics for the model on chest-Xray images: Sensitivity = 0.9913, Accuracy = 0.9883, and Dice coefficient = 0.982 (or 0.0246 in the abstract, which appears to be a typographical error given the context of high performance and Table 2). The text also notes that the model showed robust performance in handling illumination variations and hair artifacts.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep-convolutional neural network (CNN) integrated with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization

components: Two dense layers in the center of the network
architecture_details.optimization: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with Nesterov momentum

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary masks (0 or 1) for lesion segmentation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Stochastic gradient descent optimised convolutional neural network (SGD-optimized CNN)
preprocessing_techniques: Image augmentation including rotation (-40 to +40 degrees) and horizontal flipping
Threshold technique for post-processing to sharpen boundaries and remove blurry edges

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated around the range of -40 to +40 degrees.
Images were reversed around the horizontal axis.

conclusions: The proposed deep-convolutional neural network (CNN) integrated with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization and Nesterov momentum outperformed U-net and FCN architectures in medical image segmentation tasks.
The model achieved superior segmentation efficiency and boundary clarity, effectively handling complex regions of interest, varying illumination, and hair artifacts in dermoscopic images.
On the PH2 dataset for melanoma lesion segmentation, the proposed method achieved a sensitivity of 97.2%, accuracy of 98.1%, and Dice coefficient of 77.3%, surpassing U-net and FCN.

On the chest-X-ray dataset, the proposed method achieved a sensitivity of 99.3%, accuracy of 98.87%, and Dice coefficient of 98.63%, demonstrating robust performance in segmenting lung structures and lesions.
The use of a post-processing threshold technique successfully sharpened ROI boundaries and removed blurry edges, improving the visual clarity of segmented outputs for clinical interpretation.

The study concludes that the proposed technique is robust, reliable, and applicable to various medical imaging scenarios beyond the tested datasets, including potential applications for brain cancer, bladder, and Covid-19 chest-X-ray images.

Future research directions identified include the development of adaptive learning rates, mini-batch selection strategies, regularization techniques, transfer learning, and hardware acceleration to further improve segmentation accuracy and deployment efficiency.
important_remarks: The abstract reports a Dice coefficient of 0.0246 for the proposed method, which contradicts the detailed results in Table 2 (Dice = 0.9863) and the conclusion (Dice = 0.982), indicating a significant typographical error in the abstract.
The study relies on a very small dataset size (200 images for PH2 and 200 for Chest-Xray), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the findings.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between the proposed method and baselines (U-net, FCN).
The Chest-Xray dataset was sourced from Kaggle without detailed description of its origin, demographics, or annotation protocol, raising concerns about data provenance and potential bias.

The PH2 dataset was used for melanoma segmentation, but the study claims applicability to other conditions (brain cancer, bladder, Covid-19) without providing experimental evidence or validation on those specific datasets.
The paper does not report uncertainty estimates (e.g., via dropout or ensemble methods) for the segmentation predictions, which is a limitation for clinical deployment reliability.
The model architecture is extremely large (33,486,874 parameters), which may pose deployment challenges on resource-constrained devices despite the claim of efficiency.

Data leakage risk exists if the 'label mask set scope' mentioned for the test set (25%) overlaps with the training/validation splits in a non-standard way, though the paper states a 75/20/5 split for PH2.
